g.title: Sensor Housing Exploded View and Disassembly
g.desc: This diagram shows the sensor housing assembly completely taken apart. The main components are two flat wooden plates with narrow handle-like extensions and square notches along their edges; you can identify the top plate by the tactile 'PIPER' engraving. Between the plates are four identical wooden pieces shaped like hooks, each with a small circular hole. These are fastened together by two small brass screws and a smooth metal spacer tube. At the center is a small, rectangular black sensor with a thin four-wire cable (red, black, yellow, and white wires). A large, thin black rubber ring is also shown separated from the top plate. The exploded view indicates that all these pieces should be unscrewed and pulled apart.

g.title: Kit Inventory List
g.desc: This is a list of all the parts included in your kit. Please feel and count each item to ensure you have everything needed for assembly. You should have: one flat, 5mm thick plywood 'Top' piece, and one similar 'Bottom' piece which may have different cutouts. Locate four identical 5mm thick plywood 'Side' pieces. Find two small, threaded #4-40 brass '1/2 inch Screws' and one cylindrical 'Standoff' with threaded holes. Identify two flexible rubber or silicone 'O-Rings'. You will also need one 'Sensor', which is a small, flat electronic board with metal pins, and one '4-Pin Cable' composed of flexible wires with a connector. Lastly, find two 'Velcro' pieces, one with a rough hook texture and one with a soft loop texture, that stick together.

g.title: Removing Screws, Standoff, and O-Ring
g.desc: Step 1 of disassembly shows a small assembly made of two flat plywood plates—identifiable by their notched edges and the word PIPER on top—sandwiching a blue plastic component. Dashed orange arrows indicate parts to be removed. First, unthread the two metal screws from the sides of the assembly; these feel like small, thin cylinders with flat heads. Next, remove the standoff, which is a small, smooth, hollow tube located between the wooden plates. Finally, pull off the black rubber O-ring, which is a thin, flexible loop wrapped around the assembly. Place these items in your clean-up box as you remove them.

g.title: Disassembling side pieces from the base
g.desc: This step shows how to remove two small, vertical side pieces from a flat base plate. Each side piece has two tabs on its bottom edge that fit into rectangular slots on the base. One tab is straight, while the other has a small hook. To disassemble, tilt the side piece away from its hooked end and lift it upward. This action slides the notches out of the slots, as indicated by the curved dashed arrows showing the tilting motion for one piece and the vertical arrows for the piece already being lifted away.

g.title: Removing Sensor and Disconnecting 4-Pin Cable
g.desc: Identify the small, flat, rectangular electronic sensor board attached to the wider end of a T-shaped wooden base. The sensor is held on by a square velcro patch; remove it by pulling the board straight up away from the wood. Also, find the 4-pin cable with a white plastic connector plugged into the side of the sensor board. Unplug this cable by pulling the white connector horizontally away from the sensor's socket.

g.title: Disassembly Complete and Final Cleanup Instructions
g.desc: This is a concluding text panel on a blue blueprint background. The top half of the panel features large, bold white letters that read, 'DISASSEMBLY COMPLETE!' Below this, a white rounded rectangle contains smaller blue text stating, 'If not already done, place each piece on its spot in the clean up area below.' This final message indicates that all disassembly steps are finished and prompts you to organize all the wooden plywood pieces and brass hardware back into their designated storage or cleanup area.

g.title: Collect Your Kit Components
g.desc: This section helps you collect all the pieces for your kit. First, find two large, flat plywood pieces: the 'Top' piece has the word 'PIPER' cut out or engraved in its center, while the 'Bottom' piece is similar in shape but smooth in the middle. Both have four rectangular slots near the corners and two smaller rectangular slots on their longer sides. Next, locate four identical plywood 'Sides' pieces, each feeling like a small animal shape with a flat bottom and a rounded end containing a circular hole. Find two small square 'Velcro' patches, one side rough and hooked, the other soft and looped. You will need two small metal 'Screws', about half an inch long, with bumpy threads and flat heads. Locate one smooth, hollow tube, which is the 'Standoff'. Also, find one flexible, circular rubber 'O-Ring', like a small, thick rubber band. Lastly, find one long, flexible '4-Pin Cable' with four wires bundled together and a rectangular plastic connector at one end, and one small, rigid, rectangular 'Sensor' component, likely plastic, which may have tiny pins or a slot.

g.title: Top Piece
g.desc: This is a 5mm thick, flat plywood piece with an irregular shape. It has a wider, rounded rectangular body with a narrower, curved extension coming from one side. In the center of the wider body, feel for the laser-cut word 'PIPER'. Each letter, P-I-P-E-R, is a separate cutout. To orient the piece, position it so the 'PIPER' letters read left-to-right, with the curved extension on your left. You will find two small rectangular holes above the 'PIPER' text and two more rectangular holes directly below it.

g.title: Bottom Piece
g.desc: This is the 'Bottom' piece, a 5mm thick laser-cut plywood component. It feels mostly flat and rectangular. One end is narrower and smoothly rounded on both corners, like the tip of a spoon. This is the front. The piece then widens. The opposite end, the back, is wider and has two small, rounded inward curves, or 'notches', along its edge, with a flat section between them. On the wider, back half of the piece, you will feel four rectangular slots cut completely through the wood. Two slots are near one long edge and two are near the other long edge, forming a square pattern. To orient it, hold the piece so the narrower, rounded front points away from you. The wider, notched back should be closest to you, and the four rectangular slots will be on this wider half.

g.title: Plastic Side Components x 4
g.desc: You will find four identical plastic pieces. Each piece is a long, flat shape. Start from the left end; feel a rounded 'foot' extending downwards. Moving right along the bottom edge, there is a wider, shorter downward protrusion. Continue to the right end, where the piece narrows and curves upwards to a rounded tip. At this tip, you will feel a small, smooth, circular hole.

g.title: Locate Two Square Velcro Pieces
g.desc: Find two square pieces of Velcro. Each piece is approximately 3/4 inches (20 millimeters) long on each side. Unlike the smooth, rigid plywood components, these pieces feel like a soft, pliable fabric. One side of each Velcro square will feel either rough with tiny hooks or soft with tiny loops. Set both of these unique fabric squares aside with your other kit components.

g.title: Two 1/2-inch Brass #4-40 Screws
g.desc: Find two small, metallic brass screws. Each screw is about half an inch long, roughly the size of your pinky fingernail. One end has a smooth, rounded head with a single straight groove for a screwdriver. The rest of the screw is covered in fine, spiral ridges, which are the threads. These are #4-40 screws, a common small size for this kit.

g.title: One Metal Standoff
g.desc: Find one small, sturdy metal rod. Feel its sides: it has six flat surfaces or it has a smooth. Place a finger inside each end; you should feel spiral screw threads inside both openings.

g.title: Rubber O-Ring
g.desc: Find one rubber O-ring. It is a small, soft, and flexible black rubber ring, feeling like a thick, stretchy circular band. Identify it by its continuous smooth circular shape and elastic texture. It has no specific top or bottom orientation.

g.title: Single 4-Pin Grove to Male Cable
g.desc: This is a flat ribbon cable made of four thin wires joined side-by-side. On one end, there is a single rectangular plastic connector housing. On the other end, the ribbon splits into four separate wires, each ending in an individual thin metal pin. You can identify this piece by feeling the flat, ridged texture of the multi-wire ribbon and the contrast between the single plastic block on one end and the four loose wires on the other.

g.title: Electronic Sensor with Grove Connector
g.desc: This is a single, small rectangular electronic module. You can identify it tactually by feeling for a small, raised plastic box on one of its short ends, which is the connection port. The rest of the module is a flat rectangular circuit board.
